text.title: Human-in-the-Loop Design Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Human-in-the-Loop Design application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Place HITL at decision points whose stakes exceed a defined threshold (revenue, harm, legal exposure). Not 'everywhere' (cost), not 'random' (bias).
rule.rationale: Stakes-driven placement is the only defensible policy; uniform review is wasteful, random review is unjust.

rule.statement: Escalate to human when confidence < threshold OR uncertainty signals present (low judge score, retrieval miss, novelty flag).
rule.rationale: Confidence gates preserve throughput; review only the uncertain cases.

rule.statement: Every human correction is captured back into the eval set with timestamp + reviewer ID. Without capture, HITL is overhead with no compounding.
rule.rationale: Corrections are the gold-label gold mine; failing to capture them wastes the highest-quality signal you can get.

rule.statement: Review queue has an SLA (e.g., 4 business hours for urgent, 24h normal). Without SLA, the queue becomes a graveyard.
rule.rationale: Queues without SLAs accumulate; reviewers lose context; quality drops.

rule.statement: Rotate reviewers; track inter-rater agreement. Single-reviewer queues encode that reviewer's bias as ground truth.
rule.rationale: Inter-rater agreement is the calibration signal; rotation surfaces disagreement before it becomes systemic.
